concerns.text: ΔN (niche-shift magnitude) and ΔT (regional warming rate) are computed from the SAME occurrence data. FR-006 computes ΔT from 'all occurrence points of a species' while FR-005 computes ΔN from the same points. This creates a circular relationship: the predictor (ΔT) is not independent of the data used to compute the outcome (ΔN). Regional warming should be measured from an independent regional climate grid, not the species' own occurrence envelope.

Species-specific z-scoring (FR-005) makes ΔN incomparable across species. A species with narrow climate niche (small SD) will have larger standardized shift for the same absolute change. This violates the assumption that regression coefficients are comparable across taxonomic groups.

Linear regression with 10-15 species (per methodology sketch) has insufficient statistical power. With n<20, the slope estimate is highly unstable and confidence intervals will be extremely wide. This sample size cannot support the claimed inference about systematic variation across taxonomic groups.

Period definitions (1900-1950, 1970-2020) leave a 20-year gap (1950-1970) unaccounted for. The research question claims 'past century' coverage but the design has a temporal discontinuity that should be acknowledged.
